coredata.dc:title: Facile design and development of photoluminescent graphene quantum dots grafted dextran/glycol-polymeric hydrogel for thermoresponsive triggered delivery of buprenorphine on pain management in tissue implantation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology
coredata.dc:creator: Yue, Jianning
He, Liangliang
Tang, Yuanzhang
Yang, Liqiang
Wu, Baishan
Ni, Jiaxiang
coredata.dc:description: A novel nano-formulations of biocompatible, biodegradable and thermo-responsive graphene quantum dots (GQDs) loaded dextran/poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (Dex/PNIPAM) copolymeric matrix was synthesized and analyzed the materials characterization, sustained drug delivery system, tissue feasibility in the tissue implantation site. This research report was aimed to grafting and functionalizing thermo-responsive (Dex/PNIPAM) copolymeric composite with presence of graphene quantum dots to achieve thermal responsive drug delivery (TrDD) with no harm effect in the implantation site. The synthesized GQD by using ionic liquid were evaluated by spectroscopic (DLS, PL, XRD and Raman spectroscopy) and Transmission electron microscopic analysis (TEM). The ultra-small GQDs loaded Dex/PNIPAM and was appeared to be asymmetric and open uniform porous structure, which can be significantly favorable for cell uptake and greatly influenced to be an effective drug carrier into the cellular compartment with good fluid flow. The PNIPAM polymeric composite were exhibited sustained and enhanced drug release percentages with increasing temperature at above low critical solution temperature (LCST) is 39 °C comparable to the cumulative drug release profile of below LCST (32 °C), which demonstrated that thermo-responsive polymer was played a significant role in the delivery system. The treated group of GQDs-Dex/PNIPAM was observed that no inflammation and shows noteworthy stromal cell infiltration, demonstrating that the synthesized drug carriers did not harm to the nerves and tissues and only was responsible for the pain management.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pain management
Thermo-responsive
Poly(N-isopropylacrylamide)
Dextran
Graphene
Tissue implantation

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ionic Liquid Mediated Synthesis of GQDs
Preparation of GQDs Loaded Dextran/Poly(N-Isopropylacrylamide) Hydrogel
Analysis of Synthesized GQDs Assisted with IL
Investigation on Chemical Structure and Functional Groups of Composite Hydrogels

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of DP hydrogel formation loaded with GQDs.
High-resolution microscopic (TEM) images of synthesized GQDs in the absence (a) and presence of IL; (c) PL spectra of GQDs (with IL); Particles size distribution (Inserted in TEM images).
FTIR spectra (a), XRD pattern (b), Fluorescence spectra (c) and Raman spectra (d) of the synthesized GQDs in the presence and absence of IL.
Functional group interaction and chemical bonding of synthesized PNIPAM and GQDs loaded DP in the different LCST variations (above and below) was visualized by FTIR spectral analysis.
The surface morphology features and distribution of GQDs onto the prepared composite hydrogel was exhibited under microscopic (SEM & TEM) analyses with different magnifications.

Determinations of temperature dependent in vitro drug release profile of buprenorphine loaded GQDs-Dex/PNIPAM thermo-responsive hydrogel matrix under below (25 °C, 32 °C) and above LCST (39 °C), respectively.

In vitro cell compatibility evaluation of prepared dextran/PNIPAM hydrogel in the presence and absence of GQDs by using mouse fibroblast (L929) cell line in different time intervals. The fluorescence microscopic images of GQDs grafted-dextran/PNIPAM matrix treated L929fibroblast cellas exhibited green fluorescent live cells through fluorescein diacetate staining (scale bar 100 μm). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Histological study to investigated in vivo local tissue response mice treated with prepared hydrogel with and without GQDs in different time interval (scale: 100 μm).

head.ce:title: Facile design and development of photoluminescent graphene quantum dots grafted dextran/glycol-polymeric hydrogel for thermoresponsive triggered delivery of buprenorphine on pain management in tissue implantation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pain Management, Xuanwu Hospital, Capital Medical University, No. 45 Changchun Street, Xicheng, Beijing 100053, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pain Management, Xuanwu Hospital, Capital Medical University. No. 45 Changchun Street, Xicheng, Beijing China.100053.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel nano-formulations of biocompatible, biodegradable and thermo-responsive graphene quantum dots (GQDs) loaded dextran/poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (Dex/PNIPAM) copolymeric matrix was synthesized and analyzed the materials characterization, sustained drug delivery system, tissue feasibility in the tissue implantation site. This research report was aimed to grafting and functionalizing thermo-responsive (Dex/PNIPAM) copolymeric composite with presence of graphene quantum dots to achieve thermal responsive drug delivery (TrDD) with no harm effect in the implantation site. The synthesized GQD by using ionic liquid were evaluated by spectroscopic (DLS, PL, XRD and Raman spectroscopy) and Transmission electron microscopic analysis (TEM). The ultra-small GQDs loaded Dex/PNIPAM and was appeared to be asymmetric and open uniform porous structure, which can be significantly favorable for cell uptake and greatly influenced to be an effective drug carrier into the cellular compartment with good fluid flow. The PNIPAM polymeric composite were exhibited sustained and enhanced drug release percentages with increasing temperature at above low critical solution temperature (LCST) is 39 °C comparable to the cumulative drug release profile of below LCST (32 °C), which demonstrated that thermo-responsive polymer was played a significant role in the delivery system. The treated group of GQDs-Dex/PNIPAM was observed that no inflammation and shows noteworthy stromal cell infiltration, demonstrating that the synthesized drug carriers did not harm to the nerves and tissues and only was responsible for the pain management.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past decades, thermoresponsive polymeric nanomedicine that fabricated with nanomicelles, nanocapsules and nanoparticles have been received great attention in different drug delivery systems to minimize side-effects, dosage, in vivo extended circulation time, keep drug protection from biological degradation and thus to improve its therapeutic efficiency [1–3]. To approximately half of a century, polyethylene glycol (PEG) is frequently used to shelter the nanomaterials and to deliver it stealthiness, it was exposed that PEG persuades hypersensitivity reaction and complement activation in the drug delivery [4,5]. Some specific hydrophilic polysaccharides (e.g., dextran) were stated as auspicious alternatives of PEG due to their characteristic immunogenicity, biodegradable nature and bioactivity [6–8]. The nanoformulations of dextran grafts are provide the excellent colloidal stability of such nanoparticles to avert biological interactions with human cells and proteins, thus extending the half-life of nanoparticles circulation [9–11]. Recent years, biomedical researchers acquired great consideration to introduce smart or stimuli–responsive polymeric drug delivery systems (e.g., thermosensitive, pH-sensitive, photo-sensitive, molecule-responsive drug delivery systems) [12,13].

Thermal responsive drug delivery system is one of the broadly investigated areas of minimally invasive and self-administered drug delivery system for sustained drug releasing systems [14,15]. In the TrDD systems, the drug permeability through the skin can be enhanced by different external factors such as temperature, ultrasound energy, current (low voltage) and chemical enhancers. Recent researches, thermal-responsive polymers have on-demanding options in the TrDD systems to control the amount of drug delivered through skin [16,17]. Thermoresponsive polymers are called as smart polymers which display volume phase transition at a certain temperature due to their potential properties of lower and upper critical solution temperature. Thermal responsive polymers are extensively applied drug delivery of tissue engineering and pain management applications [12,18,19]. However, PNIPAM is a broadly applied thermal responsive polymeric material in various biological areas such as drug delivery, tissue engineering, sensing and cell culture applications. This thermal responsive phenomenon was highly appropriate for the controlled and remote drug delivery systems [20,21]. The encapsulated drug has been triggered and released controlled manner through LCST temperature variations of the loaded thermosensitive nanoparticles, which provides controlled drug release for the enhanced tissue penetration and potentially reduces tissue injuries of the patient [22,23].

In the past decade, quantum dots (QDs) have much attentions recently due to its lesser size properties (<10 nm) and occurred as zero dimensional nanomaterials [24,25]. As a novel type of carbon-based nanomaterials, Graphene quantum dots (QDs) are auspicious next generation carbon materials with small size in single-layer two-dimensional graphene and have huge potential in biomedical applications due to their excellent intrinsic fluorescence ability, photostability, biocompatibility, great surface area [26,27]. Importantly, graphene QDs provides higher delocalized π electrons which favorable to interaction with different functional groups (e.g., carbonyl, hydroxyl, etc.,). These properties of GQDs can be provide π-π interactions with drug molecules and have huge potential for the drug delivery system through functionalization of oxygen groups [26,28]. The ionic liquids (ILs) are one of the significant inorganic salts with relatively low melting properties and prominently it has ability to tailorable in a superficial way for different applications. Typically, ILs have great consideration in greener type organic reactions which assists as a sustainable and eco-friendly reaction medium. Recent days, room temperature ionic liquids (RTILs) have been exposed with its potential templating properties which may offer a suitable capping reaction medium for the synthesis of surface developed inorganic materials. The ILs containing heterocyclic cations and inorganic anions with diverse physic-chemical abilities could be intended into facile types of structured functional materials [29–31]. Stimulated by reported investigations, we have planned to develop a facile drug delivery system for pain management, taking high advantages of the combination effect of the light responsive graphene QDs and thermal responsive polymer. In the present research report, light responsive GQDs were synthesized and analyzed with thermal responsive PNIPAM to develop the pain management drug delivery system for the tissue recovery.

The chemicals and reagents were attained from commercial chemicals and solvents companies and used directly for the synthesis without additional purification methods. The crosslinking chemicals of N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N0-ethyl-carbodiimide hydro- chloride (EDC, 98.0%), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS,98.0) were purchased from suppliers of Aladdin Industrial Corporation, China. The precursors of PNIPAM (N-iso- propylacrylamide (>99%), potassium persulfate (A.R), methacrylic acid (>99%,)), 1-Butyl-3- methylimidazolium tetrafluoroborate room temperature ionic liquid ([bmim]BF4) were obtained and used as received from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Corporation, Shanghai. The polymer of Dextran (Mn = 70 kDa) was purchased from China suppliers of Sigma-Aldrich, China. All the other solvents and reagents were acquired from Shanghai Lanji Technology Development Co., Ltd., China and directly used as gained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ionic Liquid Mediated Synthesis of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized through Hummers method by using natural graphite with the mixture of H2SO4, KMnO4 and NaNO3 [32,33]. The quantum dots of graphene synthesis were synthesized by using oxidative cutting of prepared GO and protocol was started as per reported literature with slight modifications. The GO powder was treated with conc. Nitric acid (HNO3) and ionic liquid ([bmim]BF4); template; 2 mL, the mixture was magnetically stirred for 30 min and then kept for ultrasonication at room temperature. Then, the reaction mixture temperature was amplified to 80 °C, and the reaction process is kept at this atmosphere for 10 h. After that, the acidic nature of the reaction mixture was neutralized with sodium hydroxide (NaOH) pellets under deionized water and centrifuged at 7000 rpm. The resulting solid material was purified several times by using DI water to remove NaOH salts and dehydrated at 70 °C under air vacuum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQDs Loaded Dextran/Poly(N-Isopropylacrylamide) Hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared GQDs loaded Dextran/PNIPAM hydrogel was synthesized through the method of in situ dispersion polymerization as per the literature protocol [22,34]. In a typical synthesis, 0.4 g of N,N-methylenebis acrylamide, N-isopropylacrylamide (5 g) was mixed into a 60 mL of Milli Q ultrapure water. In additionally, 2 g of dextran polymeric solution was prepared by using DMSO (20 mL) under ultrasonication for 2 h. After that, the prepared both mixtures were mixed under nitrogen gas by using Schlenk tube and magnetically stirred at 450 rpm under temperature at 60 °C for 1 h. For the initiation of polymerization, potassium persulfate (initiator; 0.4 g) was added to the above reaction mixture. The reaction process was carried out under continuous nitrogen atmosphere at 70 °C for 20 h. After completion of polymerization, the resultant copolymer was dialyzed with DI water to remove unreacted monomer for 12 h. The prepared GQDs was fabricated onto the dextran/PNIPAM copolymeric hydrogel by using EDC-NHS (3:1) coupling agent by the activation of its carboxylic groups. The probable formation of GQDs grafted Dex/PNIPAM co-polymeric nanocomposite matrix was exhibited in the Fig. 1 as a schematic diagram.

The crystalline and phase structural analysis of IL assisted GQDs was investigated through X-ray powder (Bruker AXS D8 X-ray diffractometer) diffraction analysis by using CuKα1 radiations. The improved morphological analysis of GQDs was carried out through HR-TEM (JEOL JEM 2010; operating at 200 kV) with Gatan multi-scan CCD camera. The ultra-particles size distributions of GQDs was observed by DLS (LS Instrument AG) under diluted water. The functional group and chemical bonding interaction of synthesized GQDs and loaded hydrogel was evaluated under Fourier transform infra-red (FTIR; Perkin Elmer Spectrum 100 equipped with ATR) spectrometer. The photoluminescence properties of GQDs was observed by PL spectrum (Horiba Fluoromax-4) with a Xenon lamp as the excitation light source at atmospheric temperature. The micro-structured composite morphology was observed and exhibited by the field emission-scanning electron microscopic (SEM, Nova Nanosem 430, USA) imaging.

The in vitro cytocompatibility and toxicity ratio of the prepared GQDs loaded DP thermo-responsive hydrogel was investigated under colorimetric analysis-based method by using MTT assay. The analysis of cell viability was performed onto the mouse fibroblast cell line (L929) as per the ISO 10993-5 protocol of International Standard Cytotoxicity Test. The cells were cultured in a 96-well plates (10,000 cells/cm2) with prepared hydrogel extracts (extracted by using medium) at the appropriate incubation conditions (37 °C). After different time interval incubations, the media was changed to removing the added hydrogel extracts. After that, the prepared MTT assay (diluted with PBS, serum free and phenol red free medium) was added with the cultured cells and incubated at appropriate conditions. After 5 h, solubilized buffer prepared by Triton X-100 (10%), HCl (0.1 N) and isopropyl alcohol and mixed into the each well and again incubated for another 1 h in order to dissolve the formazan from MTT assay. The cell viability was calculated by using optical density was observed under Thermo Scientific plate reader. For the fluorescence analysis, the seeded cells with hydrogel extract in different time intervals were washed systematically by using PBS medium and stained with dye fluorescein diacetate (FDA) to display live cells. Then, they were washed roughly and observed under fluorescent microscope.

In vivo animal studies were carried out as per approved procedure of the Institutional Animal Ethics and Use Committee of the the Capital Medical University, China. Female SD rats (250 ± 10) were stored in ventilated at atmospheric temperature (27 °C ± 2 °C) and 35% humidity into the cage systems. The chronic contraction injury was created under specific surgical protocols on the right back limb of the SD rats. The animal models were anesthetized (6% isoflurane) and 10 mm skin slit was created on the medical protocol. The prepared GQDs loaded DP composited hydrogel was injected methodically over the nerve and surrounding tissues. The opened muscle layer was sutured by using vinyl sutures under intermittent suture method. The animal models were caged separately after surgical procedures and carefully observed for the further studies. The surgical site was opened after 3 days and 7 days to evaluated for histopathological observations. The sciatic and local surrounding tissues were isolated from the exact injection site of the animals. The collected tissues were poured into the buffered formalin phosphate solutions (10%) for 2 days. After that, the tissues were washed by using PBS medium and moved to ethanol (70%; histological grade). Then, paraffin entrenched sections were made from the separated tissue samples and stained with hematoxylin and eosin staining. The stained sections was photographed under Axio Scan Z1, Zeiss microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Synthesized GQDs Assisted with IL
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent graphene quantum dots have been fabricated from the graphene-based materials via sustained oxidation and reduction synthetic processes, which exhibited fine-tuned morphological structure and optical abilities depending on the ultra-small size and functional groups at the edge. The surface modified GQDs in the presence of oxygen-based functional groups and tailored optical properties was effectively prepared by the process of thermal exfoliation/oxidation strategy. The synthesized GQD by using ionic liquid were evaluated by spectroscopic (DLS, PL, XRD and Raman spectroscopy) and microscopic analysis (TEM). The DLS particles size distribution analysis was exhibited that GQDs have size populations in the ranges at 2 to 12 nm Fig. 2 (inserted). The DLS peaks displayed that the small size ratio (85%) was higher than larger size ratio (15%). However, the width and ratio of the both size distributions that are roughly gaussians, is revealed that some polydispersity occurred in particles size. The morphology structure and ultra-small particle size of GQDs have been favorable for cell uptake and greatly influenced to be an effective drug carrier. The surface morphology and ultra-small particle size was analyzed by TEM microscope, which displayed that homogeneously distributed quantum dots with spherical morphological structure with <10 nm. These synthesized ultra-small GQDs have been favorable for enter into the different human cellular compartments and biological barriers including the nucleus of cells. The consistency size of the synthesized GQD was also established by the spectroscopic method of PL spectra as shown in Fig. 2(c). The excitation of GQDs dispersions was observed at the ranges between the 310 to 370 nm and strong peak was exhibited at 560 nm. The strong photoluminescent abilities of GQDs was demonstrated that the stability and strength of the GQDs and also to be provide an active tracking system in human cells.

The analysis of the structure and functional groups of GQDs in the presence of IL was evaluated by using FT-IR spectral method (Fig. 3a). It was showed that characteristic CO, CC and CH stretching vibrations at about 1646, 1365 and 2929 cm−1, which corresponded to the GQDs formation. In addition, broad peaks around 3315 and 1024 were specified the functional groups of OH and CO, respectively. The X-ray diffraction analysis was exhibited that effective interaction of IL with the GQDs. As displayed in Fig. 3b, the characteristic peak at around 2θ = 26 (002) was confirmed the formation of GQDs. This mentioned peak has shifted to lower values and also increased intensity of peaks, which confirms the IL have influenced in the changes of interplanar spacing and oxygen containing groups of GQDs. The fluorescence study of the synthesized GQDs in the presence of IL was exhibited in Fig. 3c. The fluorescence intensity of GQDs in the absence of IL was exhibited in λem = 430 nm using a excitation (λex) of 390 nm. The formation of GQDs in the presence of IL was red shifted, which demonstrated that IL influenced into the fluorescence properties. As shown in Fig. 3 (d), Raman spectroscopic data of GQDs displays D band and G band at the range of 1320 cm−1 and 1595 cm−1, respectively. The D peak of graphene has been more intense than G peaks, which demonstrated that disordered defects after the durable oxidative treatment and graphitic structure, respectively [35–37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation on Chemical Structure and Functional Groups of Composite Hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The functional groups and bonding between the quantum dots and polymer components were demonstrated by Fourier transform infrared spectrum as shown in Fig. 4 . The spectrum of bare GQDs was displayed that the different functional chemical (hydroxyl, carbonyl and epoxy/ether) groups, which can be clarify the excellent solubility and enhanced fluorescent properties. Additionally, these functional properties have been clearly felicitated for the drug delivery systems with post-modification of target moieties. The presence of PNIPAM polymer was confirmed by the typical peaks of amide (1652 cm-1 (amide I), 1549 cm-1 (amide II) & 1280 cm-1 (amide III)) and methyl (2975, 2936, 2880 and 2852 cm−1) groups, respectively. The spectrum of dextran encapsulated PNIPAM was exhibited NH stretching (3296 cm−1) of secondary amide and bond overtone of amide II at the range of 3080 cm−1, which was demonstrated interactions between the copolymeric matrixes. As shown in spectrum of PNIPAM composite, the characteristic bands of hydrogen bonded NH stretch was greatly responded to the temperature changes, and the band frequency at 3296 cm−1 and 3080 cm−1 was shifted with corresponding to the increasing and decreasing temperature ratio of composite. Additionally, the deformation of symmetric (1388 cm−1) and asymmetric (1467 cm−1) isopropyl (CH3) functional groups and deformation of symmetric (1366 cm−1) and asymmetric (1458 cm−1) CH2 groups were established that the interactions between the GQDs and copolymer matrixes. The spectral results of the GQDs loaded DEX/PNIPAM composite matrix clearly demonstrated that the influence of organic and inorganic components and provide the confirmation of composite formation [6,38].

The morphological structure and particles distribution efficiency of GQDs loaded DEX/PNIPAM composite matrix were determined and displayed by high resolution microscopic (SEM & TEM) techniques. The images of SEM analysis are shown in Fig. 5 , which displayed that effective mesoporous morphological structured DEX/PNIPAM compared to the structure of bare DEX polymer. After addition of PNIPAM and GQDs was appeared to be asymmetric and open uniform porous structure, which can be significantly favorable for the fluid flow into the cellular compartment. This porous morphology of the matrix has been demonstrated that suitability of active drug delivery system in the pain management. The TEM analysis specified that the uniform distribution and monodispersed ultra-small GQDs onto the DEX/PNIPAM copolymeric matrix.

The drug release profile of buprenorphine loaded GQDs-Dex/PNIPAM thermo-responsive hydrogel matrix was assessed through in vitro method by using phosphate buffer saline under the appropriate biological conditions (37 °C) for 7 days as shown in Fig. 6 . To evaluate the effect of thermo-responsive polymer on drug release, buprenorphine loaded GQDs/Dex hydrogel in the absence of PNIPAM were also investigated. The cumulative buprenorphine release profile from the GQDs-Dex/PNIPAM was done at different conditions of temperature based on the increasing time interval. The PNIPAM polymeric composite were exhibited sustained and enhanced drug release percentages with increasing temperature at above LCST (39 °C) comparable to the cumulative drug release profile of below LCST (32 °C), which demonstrated that thermo-responsive polymer was played a significant role in the delivery system. Numerous influencing factors are provided by the GQDs loaded PNIPAM composite such as ratio of hydrophilic and hydrophobic components of polymeric matrixes, interactions between the drug and GQDs, temperature cycles and temperature switching to affect the size and stability of GQDs and polymeric nanocarriers and frequently release the buprenorphine from GQDs-Dex/PNIPAM. Even though the release profile remained the same, the incorporation of LNCs in the hydrogel significantly reduced the amount of drug released as a function of time indicating the ability of the hydrogel to serve as a diffusional barrier. However, the temperature triggered release was confirmed by the observation of amplified drug release where the temperature was swapped to above and below LCST at every time intervals.

The suitability and compatibility of the GQDs loaded copolymer matrix as in situ drug carriers was evaluated through in vitro cell viability analysis by using L929 fibroblast cell lines. As shown in Fig. 7 , the L929 cells were cultured with the prepared nano-polymer matrixes directly under tissue culture plates at suitable incubator (37 °C with 5% CO2) conditions. The cell viability of DEX/PNIPAM, PNIPAM and control were determined by cell counting kit in different days. The cell viability results of the composite demonstrated that effective cell viable percentage (96%) for the GQDs loaded matrix compared to the individual components. It can be established that combination inorganic counterpart with polymer matrix have been provided synergistic biological effect on the cell growth and enhanced biocompatibility. The presence of GQDs and PNIPAM components have been felicitated for the cell mechanism, due to its ability of auspicious mechanical strength on 3D cell culture and thermal sensitivity to cell growing process. The cell growth of synthesized GDQs-Dex/PNIPAM matrix was exhibited by fluorescence microscope after staining by dyes of calcein and propidium iodide (PI). The living (green fluorescence) cells are exhibited high number comparable to the dead cells (red fluorescence) on the surface nanocomposite matrix, which demonstrates the excellent biocompatibility and cell growth due to its highly porous nanocomposite.

The in vivo toxicity and tissue feasibility of the synthesized GQDs-Dex/PNIPAM matrix were analyzed by using isolated animals' sciatic nerve and adjacent tissues responses of implantations after 7th day of post-surgery. The analysis results were exhibited through H& E stained histological observations at the implantation site as shown in Fig. 8 . The treated group of GQDs-Dex/PNIPAM was observed that no inflammation and shows noteworthy stromal cell infiltration, demonstrating that the synthesized drug carriers did not harm to the nerves and tissues and only was responsible for the pain management. Additionally, the results showed that adipose tissues and muscles around harvested nerves are normal. In the present analysis, bio-degradation of prepared copolymer matrix was responsible and arbitrated by the mechanisms of enzyme lysozyme from the cells of phagocytic macrophages. The stromal cellular infiltration results from histopathology was confirmed the successive cellular degradation of synthesized matrix. The implantation site with the treated GQDs coated nano-matrix exhibited substantial infiltration of stromal cell including cells of macrophages, lymphocytes and neutrophils. These observations are demonstrated that buprenorphine loaded composite have no acute toxicity to the nerves and implantation sites and also improving anti-inflammatory efficiency. Future analysis will be study the appropriateness of these pH and thermo dual responsive degrading gels which may have the effective and sustained release purpose in the site of tissue implantation.

In summary, the ultra-small GQDs surface functionalized GQDs-Dex/PNIPAM copolymeric hydrogel was synthesized and investigated for the thermally triggered drug delivery system in pain management. The PNIPAM was grafted with dextran was provided active drug delivery profile with compatibility to the tissue implantation through its desired thermal properties of above (39 °C) and below (32 °C) LCST. The prepared GQDs-Dex/PNIPAM was evaluated and demonstrated with its comparative analysis on in in vitro and in vivo tissue feasibility analysis and temperature triggered in vitro drug release of buprenorphine drug. In vivo skin irritation analysis through H&E images were evidenced that the synthesized GQDs-Dex/PNIPAM was found to be more compatible without any harm effect of surrounding tissues. Therefore, the buprenorphine loaded GQDs-Dex/PNIPAM nanoformulated hydrogel could be a probable medical material for the thermoresponsive drug delivery for pain management in tissue implantation.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Current progress and challenges of nanoparticle-based therapeutics in pain management

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. Andreu, M. Arruebo, Current progress and challenges of nanoparticle-based therapeutics in pain management, J. Control. Release. 269 (2018) 189–213. doi:10.1016/j.jconrel.2017.11.018.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thermo-responsive magnetic Fe 3 O 4 @P(MEO 2 MA X -OEGMA 100-X) NPs and their applications as drug delivery systems

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Ghanbaja, J. Toufaily, R. Schneider, H. Alem, S. Marchal, A. Meftah, T. Hamieh, Z. Ferjaoui, E. Jamal Al Dine, T. Roques-Carmes, E. Gaffet, Thermo-responsive magnetic Fe 3 O 4 @P(MEO 2 MA X -OEGMA 100-X ) NPs and their applications as drug delivery systems, Int. J. Pharm. 532 (2017) 738–747. doi:10.1016/j.ijpharm.2017.09.019.
B. Zheng, M. Karski, S.D. Taylor, Thermoresponsive hydroxybutylated starch nanoparticles, Carbohydr. Polym. 209 (2019) 145–151. doi:10.1016/j.carbpol.2019.01.021.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Camptothecin loaded graphene oxide nanoparticle functionalized with polyethylene glycol and folic acid for anticancer drug delivery

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Deb, R. Vimala, Camptothecin loaded graphene oxide nanoparticle functionalized with polyethylene glycol and folic acid for anticancer drug delivery, J. Drug Deliv. Sci. Technol. 43 (2018) 333–342. doi:10.1016/j.jddst.2017.10.025.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A theranostic prodrug delivery system based on Pt(IV) conjugated nano-graphene oxide with synergistic effect to enhance the therapeutic efficacy of Pt drug

Li, A theranostic prodrug delivery system based on Pt(IV) conjugated nano-graphene oxide with synergistic effect to enhance the therapeutic efficacy of Pt drug, Biomaterials. 51 (2015) 12–21. doi:10.1016/j.biomaterials.2015.01.074.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nano silver decorated polyacrylamide/dextran nanohydrogels hybrid composites for drug delivery applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Prusty, S.K. Swain, Nano silver decorated polyacrylamide/dextran nanohydrogels hybrid composites for drug delivery applications, Mater. Sci. Eng. C. 85 (2018) 130–141. doi:10.1016/j.msec.2017.11.028.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-organized nanoparticle drug delivery systems from a folate-targeted dextran-doxorubicin conjugate loaded with doxorubicin against multidrug resistance

He, Self-organized nanoparticle drug delivery systems from a folate-targeted dextran-doxorubicin conjugate loaded with doxorubicin against multidrug resistance, RSC Adv. 5 (2015) 71164–71173. doi:10.1039/c5ra10341j.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biocompatible polyethylenimine-graft-dextran catiomer for highly efficient gene delivery assisted by a nuclear targeting ligand

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dextran coated Fe 3 O 4 nanoparticles as a near-infrared laser-driven photothermal agent for efficient ablation of cancer cells in vitro and in vivo

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Xi, H. Bai, J. Ma, Y. Gao, W. Li, F. Wang, X. Li, W. Liu, Dextran coated Fe 3 O 4 nanoparticles as a near-infrared laser-driven photothermal agent for efficient ablation of cancer cells in vitro and in vivo, Mater. Sci. Eng. C. 90 (2018) 46–56. doi:10.1016/j.msec.2018.04.030.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chloroquine diphosphate bearing dextran nanoparticles augmented drug delivery and overwhelmed drug resistance in plasmodium falciparum parasites

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Kashyap, R. Kaur, A. Baldi, U.K. Jain, R. Chandra, J.
